clue: A former corset maker's apprentice opened a house here in 1910, dressing Parisian women in jersey and freeing them from the waist down.
source_note: Gabrielle Chanel opened her first millinery shop on Rue Cambon, Paris, in 1910.

clue: In 1999, Alexander McQueen sent models down a runway soaked with water, while robots spray-painted their white dresses yellow and black.
source_note: The 'No. 13' show took place at Victoria Miro Gallery, London. The robots were borrowed from Fiat.

clue: The largest collection of haute couture in the world lives in a former train station, with roughly 10,000 garments archived in acid-free boxes.
source_note: The MAD Paris holds the Palais Galliera overflow, plus its own textile collection dating to the 16th century.

clue: Yves Saint Laurent was born in this North African port city — a place whose light and color would later saturate his entire palette.
source_note: YSL was born in Oran on August 1, 1936. He later opened a villa in Marrakech, which also deeply influenced his work.

clue: The Bauhaus school opened its doors here in 1919, collapsing the boundary between fine art and craft, and between a chair and a sculpture.
source_note: Walter Gropius founded the Bauhaus in Weimar. It moved to Dessau in 1925 and Berlin in 1932 before being shut down by the Nazis.

clue: Cristóbal Balenciaga opened his Paris atelier in 1937 after fleeing the Spanish Civil War — but he had already been dressing royalty here for a decade.
source_note: Balenciaga opened his first house in San Sebastián (Donostia) in 1917 at age 22, dressing the Spanish aristocracy.

clue: Comme des Garçons launched here before conquering Paris, with early collections so avant-garde the local press didn't know what to make of them.
source_note: Rei Kawakubo founded Comme des Garçons in Tokyo in 1969. The Paris debut came in 1981 and shocked the fashion establishment.

clue: Andy Warhol's Factory wasn't just a studio — it was a philosophy made concrete in a building in a city that never stops performing.
source_note: The original Factory was at 231 East 47th Street. Warhol moved the operation three times between 1963 and 1984.

clue: Hussein Chalayan buried garments in a friend's garden in this city, then dug them up for his graduate show — the rust and rot became the collection.
source_note: Chalayan's 1993 Central Saint Martins graduate show 'The Tangent Flows' featured clothes buried with iron filings in a garden in Palmers Green.

clue: Frida Kahlo was born and died in the same cobalt-blue house, which now bears her name and anchors the neighborhood her pain made famous.
source_note: The Casa Azul (Blue House) in Coyoacán is now the Museo Frida Kahlo. She was born there in 1907 and died there in 1954.

clue: Prada began not as a fashion house but as a leather goods shop opened here in 1913, selling luggage to the city's wealthy travelers.
source_note: Mario Prada opened Fratelli Prada in the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II in 1913. The fashion line didn't begin until Miuccia took over in 1978.

clue: De Stijl — the movement that gave us Mondrian's grids and Rietveld's red-blue chair — was founded and centered in this Dutch city.
source_note: The De Stijl journal was first published in Leiden in 1917. Piet Mondrian, Theo van Doesburg, and Gerrit Rietveld were key figures.

clue: Madeleine Vionnet — who invented the bias cut — ran her atelier here from 1912, employing up to 1,200 people at the height of her career.
source_note: Vionnet's atelier was on Avenue Montaigne. She fingerprinted every label to prevent counterfeiting — an early IP innovator.

clue: The world's only museum dedicated entirely to lace opened in a city that made the craft synonymous with its name for five centuries.
source_note: The Lace Centre (Kantcentrum) in Bruges has been teaching and preserving bobbin lace since 1717. Bruges lace was famous across European courts.

clue: Karl Lagerfeld once turned the Grand Palais into a supermarket for Chanel's runway — aisles of branded goods, fluorescent lights and all.
source_note: The Chanel AW 2014 show transformed the Grand Palais into 'Chanel Shopping Center.' Guests received Chanel-branded grocery bags.

clue: Isamu Noguchi was born in this American city in 1904 to a Japanese poet and an American writer — a duality that would define every sculpture he made.
source_note: Noguchi was born in Los Angeles — but his studio and museum are in Long Island City, New York, which is where his legacy is most physically anchored.

clue: Vivienne Westwood's first shop opened on the King's Road here, selling clothes that would ignite punk fashion under a name that changed several times.
source_note: 430 King's Road, known as 'Let it Rock', then 'Too Fast to Live', then 'SEX', was the nucleus of British punk fashion from 1971.

clue: Memphis Group's maximalist, anti-minimalist furniture burst onto the design world from this Italian design capital in 1981.
source_note: Ettore Sottsass founded Memphis Group in Milan. Their first show was at the 1981 Milan Furniture Fair, and it changed design forever.

clue: The Costume Institute — home to the Met Gala's annual exhibition — lives inside this museum that straddles two zip codes on Fifth Avenue.
answer_label: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
source_note: The Costume Institute was founded in 1937 as the Museum of Costume Art. It merged with the Met in 1946 and is now one of the world's premier fashion archives.

clue: Elsa Schiaparelli set up her Paris atelier in the Place Vendôme in 1934, where she collaborated with Dalí and made a lobster a fashion statement.
source_note: The lobster dress (1937), made in collaboration with Salvador Dalí, is one of the most iconic objects in fashion history. It's currently in the Philadelphia Museum of Art.
